title: The Sixth Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## February 16, 2020 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was delivered on the Sixth Sunday after the Epiphany, falling within the season of Ordinary Time following Christmas. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), the readings would have been Deuteronomy 30:15-20, Psalm 119:1-8, 1 Corinthians 3:1-9, and Matthew 5:21-37, with the Matthew passage likely being the focus as it continues Jesus's teachings from the Sermon on the Mount. This falls in Year A of the three-year lectionary cycle, featuring the Gospel of Matthew.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in February 2020 was in a relatively stable period between General Conventions, though the denomination continued to navigate post-2018 Convention decisions on LGBTQ+ inclusion and prayer book revision. The Diocese of Colorado, serving the Denver metropolitan area and surrounding regions, was under the leadership of Bishop Kimberly Chastain, who had taken office in 2018. No major diocesan crises or special elections were occurring at this specific moment, though the national church was watching developments in the Church of England regarding similar LGBTQ+ issues.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver weather in mid-February was typical for the season with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and occasional snow. The Colorado legislature was in active session (it begins in early January), with discussions ongoing about education funding and healthcare issues. No major local crises dominated the Capitol Hill or downtown Denver news in the week prior to February 16. The city was focused on typical winter civic business and preparation for spring events.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Democratic primary race was in full swing following Iowa's caucuses on February 3 and New Hampshire's primary on February 11, with Senator Bernie Sanders gaining momentum and Michael Bloomberg's late entry creating significant campaign headlines. The Trump administration was in the midst of impeachment trial proceedings in the Senate (the acquittal vote occurred February 5, 2020), making politics intensely polarized. The stock market was performing well, with no indication yet of the COVID-19 pandemic's economic impact, though there were growing concerns about the virus's spread from China (the first COVID death in the US would occur nine days later on February 25).

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating in China and beginning to spread globally, with the World Health Organization expressing grave concern about the virus's trajectory, though most Americans remained largely unaware of its eventual severity. Iran was dealing with aftermath of the January 3 Soleimani assassination and January 8 missile strikes on US bases, with regional tensions still elevated. The Australian bushfire season had recently concluded (ending in February), leaving the country in a state of environmental and economic reckoning with unprecedented fire damage.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America in mid-February 2020 was marked by intense political polarization centered on the impeachment trial, primary election fever, and deepening partisan divisions. The cultural mood was energized by the primary race but also weary from political conflict; there was widespread optimism about the economy and job market. Sports culture was anticipating March Madness and the lead-up to the 2020 Olympics scheduled for Tokyo (though cancellation was months away from being discussed). No one in Denver's congregations could have known that within weeks, the COVID-19 pandemic would transform everything about gathering, worship, and community life—making this one of the last "normal" Sundays in American churches for many months to come.
